title: Heck and Hess Questionnaire
subtitle: Neuroticism screening
description: 40 statements on emotional reactivity. Answer Yes or No about yourself.
algorithm: 1. You are given a list of statements.
2. For each statement, answer Yes if you agree (consider it true for you) or No if you disagree.
comment: If you have any concerns, consult a qualified specialist.
questions: Do you feel inwardly tense?
I am often so absorbed in something that I cannot fall asleep.
I feel easily hurt.
I find it hard to talk to strangers.
Do you often feel indifferent and tired for no particular reason?
I often feel that people are critically examining me.
Are you often troubled by useless thoughts that will not leave your head even when you try?
I am quite nervous.
I feel that no one understands me.
I am quite irritable.
If people were not against me, my affairs would go more successfully.
I take unpleasant things too close to heart for too long.
Even the thought of possible failure worries me.
I have had very strange and unusual experiences.
Do you sometimes feel joyful, sometimes sad for no apparent reason?
Throughout the day I daydream and fantasize more than I should.
Is your mood easily changed?
I often struggle with myself not to show my shyness.
I would like to be as happy as other people seem to be.
I sometimes tremble or have chills.
Does your mood often change with or without a serious reason?
Do you sometimes feel afraid even when there is no real danger?
Criticism or reprimand hurts me deeply.
Sometimes I am so restless that I cannot sit still.
Do you sometimes worry too much about minor things?
I often feel dissatisfied.
I find it hard to concentrate on a task or work.
I do many things I later regret.
For the most part I am happy.
I am not confident enough in myself.
I suffer from a sense of inferiority.
Sometimes I feel completely worthless.
I often feel simply awful.
I analyze myself a lot.
Sometimes everything hurts.
I have oppressive moods.
Something is wrong with my nerves.
I find it hard to keep a conversation going when meeting someone.
The hardest struggle for me is the struggle with myself.
Do you sometimes feel that difficulties are great and insurmountable?

title: Hare Test
subtitle: Psychopathy screening
description: Screens interpersonal and behavioral patterns. Answer honestly—there are no right answers.
algorithm: 1. You are given a list of statements.
2. For each statement, give one of the suggested answers.
comment: If you have any concerns, consult a qualified specialist.
questions: I am never tongue-tied.
In important relationships, I am above most people.
I am prone to boredom.
I lie to smooth things over.
I deceive people in some matters.
I rarely feel guilty.
I am an emotional person.
I rarely become emotionally attached to others.
Others often pay for me.
I am impatient.
I lead a disorderly sex life.
I was a difficult child.
I find it hard to stay committed to long-term goals.
I am impulsive.
I often do work carelessly.
I try to avoid responsibility.
My romantic relationships usually fall apart quickly.
I committed several crimes as a juvenile.
I violated probation.
I committed several types of crimes.
I am neither shy nor timid; I speak with authority and confidence.
I am exceptional.
I need risk to feel alive.
I am mostly an honest person.
I feel bad when I deceive people.
If someone deserves punishment, I do not feel too bad about it.
I think strong emotions are for the weak.
I think if people get offended, that is their problem.
I always look out for myself.
I never act rashly.
I think sex should not be taken lightly.
I often got into trouble at school.
I lack direction in my life.
I never give in to temptation.
I always keep my word.
My problems are mostly other people's fault.
I do not like commitments in relationships.
I was a bully at school.
I showed disrespect to the court.
I would not be proud of having committed crimes.

title: L.G. Pochebut Questionnaire
subtitle: Aggressiveness screening
description: Estimate how often aggressive reactions show up in everyday situations.
algorithm: 1. You are given a list of statements.
2. For each statement, give one of the suggested answers.
comment: If you have any concerns, consult a qualified specialist.
questions: During an argument I often raise my voice.
If someone annoys me, I may say everything I think about them.
If I need physical force to defend my rights, I will use it without hesitation.
When I meet someone unpleasant, I may pinch or push them unnoticed.
Caught up in an argument, I may bang my fist on the table to get attention or prove I am right.
I constantly feel that others do not respect my rights.
Remembering the past, I sometimes feel hurt for myself.
Though I do not show it, envy sometimes gnaws at me.
If I disapprove of acquaintances' behavior, I tell them directly.
In strong anger I use harsh words and swear.
If someone raises a hand against me, I try to strike first.
I sometimes get so furious that I throw things.
I often feel the need to rearrange furniture or completely change it.
In communication I often feel like a powder keg ready to explode.
I sometimes feel like playing a mean joke on someone.
When I am angry, I usually become gloomy.
In conversation I try to listen carefully without interrupting.
In my youth my fists often itched and I was always ready to use them.
If I know someone deliberately pushed me, it may come to a fight.
Creative clutter on my desk helps me work effectively.
I remember being so angry that I grabbed whatever was at hand and broke it.
Sometimes people irritate me just by being present.
I am often surprised by hidden motives behind someone's good deeds toward me.
If someone offends me, I lose the desire to talk to anyone.
I sometimes deliberately say nasty things about people I dislike.
When furious, I shout the nastiest insults.
In childhood I avoided fighting.
I know when and why it is okay to hit someone.
When angry, I may slam the door.
It seems to me that people around me do not like me.
I constantly share my feelings and experiences with others.
Very often my words and actions harm me.
When people yell at me, I yell back.
If someone hits me first, I hit them back.
It irritates me when things are not in their place.
If I cannot fix a broken item, in anger I destroy it completely.
Other people always seem more successful to me.
When I think of someone I dislike, I may get excited with the desire to harm them.
Sometimes it seems fate played a cruel joke on me.
If someone treats me badly, I become very upset about it.

title: GAD-7 Scale
subtitle: Anxiety screening
description: Seven questions about how often you were bothered by each symptom over the last two weeks.
algorithm: 1. Think about the last two weeks.
2. For each item, choose how often you were bothered by the symptom.
comment: This is a screening tool, not a diagnosis. Seek professional help if anxiety is severe.
questions: Feeling nervous, anxious, or on edge
Not being able to stop or control worrying
Worrying too much about different things
Trouble relaxing
Being so restless that it is hard to sit still
Becoming easily annoyed or irritable
Feeling afraid as if something awful might happen

title: K10 Scale
subtitle: Psychological distress
description: Ten questions about how often you experienced each state during the last four weeks.
algorithm: 1. Think about the last four weeks.
2. For each item, choose how often it was true for you.
comment: This is a screening tool, not a diagnosis. Seek support if distress is high.
questions: Tired out for no good reason
Nervous
So nervous that nothing could calm you down
Hopeless
Restless or fidgety
So restless you could not sit still
Depressed
Everything was an effort
So sad that nothing could cheer you up
Worthless

title: WHO-5 Index
subtitle: Subjective well-being
description: Five questions about positive well-being over the last two weeks. A higher total score means higher subjective well-being.
algorithm: 1. Think about the last two weeks.
2. For each item, mark how often you felt the way described.
comment: This screens subjective well-being; it is not a diagnosis.
questions: I have felt cheerful and in good spirits
I have felt calm and relaxed
I have felt active and vigorous
I woke up feeling fresh and rested
My daily life has been filled with things that interest me

title: PHQ-9 Scale
subtitle: Depression screening
description: Nine questions about depressive symptoms over the last two weeks. Rate how often each symptom bothered you.
algorithm: 1. Think about the last two weeks.
2. For each item, choose how often you were bothered by the symptom.
comment: This is a screening tool, not a diagnosis. Seek professional help if symptoms are severe.
questions: Little interest or pleasure in doing things
Feeling down, depressed, or hopeless
Trouble falling or staying asleep, or sleeping too much
Feeling tired or having little energy
Poor appetite or overeating
Feeling bad about yourself, or that you are a failure or have let others down
Trouble concentrating on things, such as reading or watching TV
Moving or speaking slowly, or being fidgety or restless
Thoughts that you would be better off dead or of hurting yourself

title: ISI-7 Index
subtitle: Insomnia severity
description: Seven questions about sleep problems over the last two weeks. Rate the severity of each difficulty.
algorithm: 1. Think about the last two weeks.
2. For each item, choose how much the described problem bothered you.
comment: This screens for insomnia; it is not a diagnosis. Seek help if sleep problems persist.
questions: Difficulty falling asleep
Difficulty staying asleep
Problems waking up too early
How dissatisfied are you with your current sleep pattern
How noticeable to others do you think your sleep problems are
How worried or distressed are you about your current sleep problems
How much do your sleep problems interfere with daily life

title: ESS-8 Scale
subtitle: Daytime sleepiness
description: Eight everyday situations. Rate how likely you would be to doze off in each.
algorithm: 1. Imagine each situation listed.
2. Choose how likely you would be to doze off in that situation.
comment: This screens for daytime sleepiness; it is not a diagnosis. Consult a doctor if sleepiness is severe.
questions: Sitting and reading
Watching TV
Sitting inactive in a public place
As a passenger in a car for an hour without a break
Lying down to rest in the afternoon
Sitting and talking to someone
Sitting quietly after lunch without alcohol
In a car, stopped for a few minutes in traffic

title: PHQ-15 Scale
subtitle: Somatic symptoms
description: Fifteen questions about physical symptoms over the last four weeks. Rate how much each symptom bothered you.
algorithm: 1. Think about the last four weeks.
2. For each item, choose how much the symptom bothered you.
comment: This screens somatic symptoms; it is not a diagnosis. See a doctor if symptoms persist.
questions: Stomach pain
Back pain
Pain in your arms, legs, or joints
Menstrual cramps or other problems with your periods
Headaches
Chest pain
Dizziness
Fainting spells
Feeling your heart pound or race
Shortness of breath
Pain or problems during sexual intercourse
Constipation, loose bowels, or diarrhea
Nausea, gas, or indigestion
Feeling tired or having low energy
Trouble sleeping

title: SCOFF Questionnaire
subtitle: Eating disorders screening
description: Five brief questions to screen for possible eating disorders. Answer yes or no.
algorithm: 1. Read each question carefully.
2. Answer yes if the statement is true for you, or no if it is not.
comment: This is a screening tool, not a diagnosis. Seek professional help if the screen is positive.
questions: Do you make yourself Sick because you feel uncomfortably full?
Do you worry you have lost Control over how much you eat?
Have you recently lost more than 14 lb (6 kg) in a three-month period?
Do you believe yourself to be Fat when others say you are too thin?
Would you say Food dominates your life?

title: SWLS Scale
subtitle: Life satisfaction
description: Five statements about life satisfaction. A higher total score means higher subjective satisfaction.
algorithm: 1. Read each statement.
2. Choose how much you agree with it.
comment: This screens subjective life satisfaction; it is not a diagnosis.
questions: In most ways my life is close to my ideal
The conditions of my life are excellent
I am satisfied with my life
So far I have gotten the important things I want in life
If I could live my life over, I would change almost nothing
